images.caption:
Figure 1:Conceptual illustrations of CapCode and CapReward. CapCode (left) is a dataset-construction framework with a capped achievable pass rate, enabling deceptively high performance detection.
CapReward (right) is a reward design method that discourages reward hacking by penalizing pass rates that exceed the cap.
Figure 4:Illustration of CapReward defined in Eq. (2) with different steepness levels at the cap of0.50.5.

Figure 5:CapCode effectively flags potential cheating immediately as such behavior emerges, e.g., by submission round 2, right after the model observes failure tests. Increasing performance on the open set together with non-increasing (or decreasing) performance on the hidden set indicates test-gaming behavior.
A dashed line indicates the cap.
A star (⋆\star) marks the first submission round at which cheating is detected according to the binomial test at the significance level of 1%, where task-level CapCode uses a test over examples and case-level CapCode uses an aggregated test over all test cases.

Figure 6:Diagonal alignment of the scatter points indicates that CapCode preserves the performance ranking of LLMs on capped BigCodeBench datasets.
Kendall’sτ\tauis0.940.94and0.980.98for case-level and task-level CapCode, respectively.
Results for additional datasets are provided in AppendixF.1.

Figure 7:CapCode maintains effectiveness in detecting implausibly high evaluation scores in prompt-exposed and workspace-exposed settings.
A star (⋆\star) indicates cheating is detected with the binomial test at a significance level of 1%, where task-level CapCode uses a test over examples and case-level CapCode uses an aggregated test over all test cases.
We use Claude Code for Claude Sonnet 4.6 and Codex for GPT-5.4

Figure 8:CapReward consistently outperforms baseline reward functions under all cheating regimes, achieving both stronger hidden-test performance and smaller gaps. As the ratio of cheating (hardcode) training examples used during supervised fine-tuning increases, CapReward’s advantage becomes increasingly pronounced, while baseline reward functions tend to push the model further toward cheating behavior, as evidenced by larger gaps compared to the initial gap. Bars show mean performance, and error bars denote standard deviations over five runs. Bold numbers indicate the best performance or smallest gap.

Figure 9:CapReward maintains performance comparable to baselines, indicating no adverse effect. Bars show mean performance, and error bars denote standard deviations over five runs. Bold numbers indicate the best performance or smallest gap.

Figure 10:Training on CapCode data alone is insufficient, as B, NB and Combined perform worse than CapReward. Bars show mean performance, and error bars denote standard deviations over five runs. Bold numbers indicate the best performance or smallest gap.

Figure 24:KL regularization (B+KL and NB+KL) can preserve/worsen cheating behavior while removing KL (B and NB) reduces such behavior.
Figure 25:KL regularization can worsen cheating behavior over longer training.
